definition: Whole numbers that follow each other in order, each one greater than the previous. Used to locate irrational numbers between two whole-number bounds.
example_1: 5, 6, 7 or 11, 12, 13 are consecutive integers.
example_2: The square root of 20 falls between consecutive integers 4 and 5, since 4²=16 and 5²=25.
key_rule: To trap an irrational root, find the two perfect squares it falls between, then take their roots.
memory_hook: Whole numbers, one after another.

definition: A number whose decimal form never terminates and never repeats. It cannot be written as a fraction of two integers.
example_1: Pi = 3.14159265... — the digits never repeat.
example_2: Square roots of non-perfect-squares are irrational, like √2, √3, √5.
key_rule: Rational decimals stop or repeat; irrational decimals never do either.
memory_hook: Never ends, never repeats, never a fraction.

definition: A number that equals an integer multiplied by itself. The square root of a perfect square is a whole number.
example_1: 16 is a perfect square because 4×4=16.
example_2: 20 is NOT a perfect square — √20 ≈ 4.47, irrational.
key_rule: The square root of a perfect square is always a whole number.
memory_hook: A whole number times itself.

definition: A rational number (fraction or decimal) used to estimate an irrational number. Found by squeezing the value between closer and closer rational bounds.
example_1: √2 is between 1 and 2, then between 1.4 and 1.5, narrowing to about 1.41.
example_2: Square the guess, compare to the target, then narrow the range one decimal place at a time.
key_rule: Squeeze the irrational number between closer and closer rational bounds.
memory_hook: Get closer and closer with decimals.

definition: Any number that can be written as a ratio p/q of two integers, where q is not zero. Includes integers, fractions, and terminating or repeating decimals.
example_1: 3/4, -5/8, and 2/3 are all rational.
example_2: 0.25 = 1/4 and 0.333... = 1/3 are also rational.
key_rule: If it can be written as p/q, it's rational — this includes repeating and terminating decimals.
memory_hook: Any fraction of two integers.

definition: A decimal in which a digit or block of digits repeats infinitely. Shown with a bar over the repeating part. Repeating decimals are rational numbers.
example_1: 0.333... is written 0.3 with a bar — the 3 repeats.
example_2: 0.142857142857... has a 6-digit repeating block.
key_rule: Both terminating and repeating decimals are rational.
memory_hook: The bar shows what repeats forever.

definition: A decimal that ends after a finite number of digits. A fraction terminates when its denominator (in lowest terms) has only 2s and 5s as prime factors.
example_1: 1 ÷ 4 = 0.25, stopping after 2 places.
example_2: 1/3 = 0.333... never stops, since 3 isn't a factor of 2 or 5.
key_rule: Check the denominator's prime factors — only 2s and 5s mean it terminates.
memory_hook: It stops, because the denominator only has 2s and 5s.

definition: Describes a portion of a graph where the output falls as the input increases, reading from left to right. A negative slope indicates a decreasing line.
example_1: As x goes right, y goes down.
example_2: Negative slope means decreasing.
key_rule: A decreasing function's graph falls as you read left to right.
memory_hook: Line goes down to the right.

definition: A rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. No input may produce more than one output.
example_1: f(x) = 2x + 1: input x=3 gives exactly one output, 7.
example_2: An input pointing to two different outputs is NOT a function.
key_rule: Every input must map to exactly one output for it to be a function.
memory_hook: One input, one output — always.

definition: Describes a portion of a graph where the output rises as the input increases, reading from left to right. A positive slope indicates an increasing line.
example_1: As x goes right, y goes up.
example_2: Positive slope means increasing.
key_rule: An increasing function's graph rises as you read left to right.
memory_hook: Line goes up to the right.

definition: The output of a function when the input is zero, representing the starting amount. It equals the y-intercept of a linear function.
example_1: In f(x) = 10x + 20, the initial value is 20 (the cost before any months).
example_2: It's the same as the y-value when x = 0 in a table, or the y-intercept on a graph.
key_rule: The initial value is the same as the y-intercept, b.
memory_hook: What you start with, before anything changes.

definition: The value supplied to a function, usually the x-value or independent variable. It is the number placed into the function rule.
example_1: In f(4), the 4 is the input.
example_2: In a table, the x-column holds the inputs.
key_rule: The input is the value you choose and feed into the function.
memory_hook: What you put in — the x-value.

definition: A function whose graph is a straight line and whose rate of change is constant. It can be written in the form y equals m x plus b.
example_1: Equal steps in x (+2, +2, +2) produce a straight line.
example_2: f(x) = mx + b is the standard form of a linear function.
key_rule: A linear function has a constant slope and a straight-line graph.
memory_hook: Straight line, steady rate of change.

definition: Describes a function whose graph is not a straight line and whose rate of change is not constant. The differences between successive outputs are unequal.
example_1: Differences of +3, +5, +7 are unequal, showing a nonlinear pattern.
example_2: A curved graph is nonlinear.
key_rule: Unequal differences in a table signal a nonlinear function.
memory_hook: Curved graph, changing rate.

definition: The result a function produces, usually the y-value or dependent variable. It is the number that comes out after applying the function rule.
example_1: f(4) = 9 means the output is 9.
example_2: In a table, the y-column holds the outputs.
key_rule: The output is the result after the function rule is applied.
memory_hook: What comes out — the y-value.

definition: How much the output quantity changes per unit change in the input, found as change in y divided by change in x. For a line it equals the slope.
example_1: A plant grows 3 cm per week — rate of change = 3 cm/week.
example_2: Rate of change = change in y ÷ change in x.
key_rule: For a linear function, rate of change is constant and equals the slope.
memory_hook: How much y changes for each step in x.

definition: Any set of ordered pairs linking inputs to outputs. A function is a relation in which each input has exactly one output.
example_2: Every function is a relation, but not every relation is a function.
key_rule: A relation becomes a function only when each input has exactly one output.
memory_hook: Any pairing of inputs and outputs.

definition: A way of showing a function, such as a verbal description, an equation, a table, or a graph. Different representations of the same function are equivalent.
example_1: 'Output is one more than twice the input' describes the same function as f(x) = 2x + 1.
example_2: A table and a graph can represent the exact same rule.
key_rule: You can translate between words, equations, tables, and graphs — they describe the same rule.
memory_hook: Same function, four different looks.

definition: The constant rate of change of a linear function, equal to rise over run. It tells how much the output changes for each unit increase in the input.
example_1: In f(x) = 2x + 1, the slope is 2 — output rises 2 each time input rises 1.
example_2: Slope = rise ÷ run.
key_rule: Slope is the constant rate of change for a linear function.
memory_hook: How steep the line is.

definition: A formula for finding the slope between two points, equal to the difference in y-values divided by the difference in x-values, taken in the same order.
example_2: Keep the order consistent — top and bottom must subtract in the same direction.
key_rule: Subtract the y-values and x-values in the same order, then divide.
memory_hook: Rise over run, from any two points.

definition: A point where a graph changes direction, switching from increasing to decreasing or from decreasing to increasing. Straight lines have none.
example_1: A graph that goes up then down has a turning point at the top.
example_2: Turning points appear in nonlinear graphs, not straight lines.
key_rule: Linear graphs have no turning points — they go one direction forever.
memory_hook: Where the graph switches direction.

definition: The point where a graph crosses the x-axis, where the y-value equals zero. Found by setting y to zero and solving for x.
example_1: For y = 2x - 6: set y=0, solve 0=2x-6, x=3. X-intercept is (3, 0).
example_2: The x-intercept always has a y-coordinate of 0.
key_rule: Set y = 0 and solve for x to find the x-intercept.
memory_hook: Where the line crosses the x-axis.

definition: The point where a function's graph crosses the y-axis, equal to the output when the input is zero. In a linear function it is the initial value b.
example_1: In f(x) = 2x + 1, the y-intercept is 1, since f(0) = 1.
example_2: The y-intercept is the starting amount before any change.
key_rule: Set x = 0 to find the y-intercept.
memory_hook: Where the line crosses the y-axis.

definition: In scientific notation, the decimal number multiplied by a power of ten. It must be greater than or equal to one and less than ten.
example_1: 3.6 × 10⁵: 3.6 is a valid coefficient (between 1 and 10).
example_2: 36 × 10⁴ is NOT valid — 36 is too big.
key_rule: The coefficient must be at least 1 and less than 10.
memory_hook: The front number, always between 1 and 10.

definition: The constant ratio k in a proportional relationship y equals k times x. It equals the unit rate and the slope of the graph.
example_1: If 4 apples cost 12 dollars, k = 12/4 = 3 dollars per apple.
example_2: On the graph, the line rises 3 for every 1 across — k is the slope.
key_rule: k is the unit rate, the slope, AND the value of y when x=1 — all the same number.
memory_hook: k = y/x, always.

definition: A condition or limit in a problem, such as a total number or total cost, that is translated into an equation. A system of equations encodes all the constraints.
example_1: '10 tickets sold' becomes the equation a + c = 10.
example_2: 'Total of 68 dollars' becomes 8a + 5c = 68.
key_rule: Each constraint in a word problem becomes one equation.
memory_hook: A real-world limit, turned into an equation.

definition: Confirming that a solution is reasonable and valid within the real-world situation of the problem, such as being positive, a whole number, or a sensible size.
example_1: 6 miles at a taxi rate checks out: 2(6)+3=15 dollars matches the given cost.
example_2: A solution of x = -4 buses fails the context check — you can't have negative buses.
key_rule: After solving, ask: is the answer positive, whole, and reasonable in size?
memory_hook: Does the answer make sense in real life?

definition: A value that, used three times as a factor, gives the original number. It is the inverse of cubing and equals the edge length of a cube with that volume.
example_1: Cube root of 64 = 4, since 4×4×4=64.
example_2: The cube root of a perfect cube is a whole number.
key_rule: Cube root undoes cubing.
memory_hook: What number times itself three times gives this?

definition: Clearly state what an unknown letter represents, including its units, before writing an equation. This is the first step in solving a word problem.
example_1: Let x = the number of miles.
example_2: 'Let x = the taxi' is vague; 'let x = number of miles' is clear.
key_rule: Always write 'Let x = ...' with units.
memory_hook: Say exactly what the letter stands for.

definition: Assigning a distinct letter to each unknown quantity, with units, before setting up the equations of a system from a word problem.
example_1: a = number of adult tickets, c = number of child tickets.
example_2: Then write one equation per condition: a+c=10 and 8a+5c=68.
key_rule: Name each unknown with its own letter and units before writing equations.
memory_hook: One letter for each unknown.

definition: Multiplying a factor by a sum or difference inside parentheses by multiplying it by each term separately. a times the quantity b plus c equals ab plus ac.
key_rule: Distribute the outside factor to every term inside — don't forget the second term.
memory_hook: Multiply the outside number by everyone inside.

definition: A method for solving a system by adding or subtracting the equations to cancel one variable, leaving a single-variable equation to solve.
example_1: 3x+y=10 and 2x-y=5: adding cancels y, giving 5x=15, x=3.
example_2: If needed, multiply an equation first to create opposite coefficients.
key_rule: Line up the equations and add or subtract so one variable cancels.
memory_hook: Add or subtract to cancel a variable.

definition: A mathematical statement that two expressions are equal, shown with an equals sign. The goal is usually to find the value of the variable that makes it true.
example_1: 2x + 3 = 11 is an equation.
example_2: 2x + 3 (no equals sign) is only an expression, not an equation.
key_rule: Whatever you do to one side, you must do to the other to keep it balanced.
memory_hook: Two expressions, balanced by an equals sign.

definition: An equation that holds true for all values of the variable because both sides are equivalent expressions. It has infinitely many solutions.
example_1: 3(x+2) = 3x+6 simplifies to 3x+6 = 3x+6 — true for every x.
example_2: Try x=0: 6=6. Try x=5: 21=21. Always true.
key_rule: An identity simplifies to the same expression on both sides.
memory_hook: True no matter what x is.

definition: Occurs when solving an equation eliminates the variable and leaves a true statement. Every value of the variable works; both sides are the same line.
example_1: 2(x+3) = 2x+6 simplifies to 6=6 — always true.
example_2: Graphically, both equations are the same line.
key_rule: A true statement with no variable left means infinitely many solutions.
memory_hook: Variable disappears, statement is true — infinite solutions.

definition: An exponent that is a whole number, which may be positive, zero, or negative. Positive means repeated multiplication, zero gives one, and negative gives a reciprocal.
example_2: As the exponent decreases by 1, the value is divided by the base.
key_rule: The pattern of dividing by the base is continuous through zero and into negatives.
memory_hook: Positive, zero, or negative — the pattern keeps going.

definition: To translate the numerical solution of a system back into a meaningful statement about the original real-world situation, including units.
example_1: a=6, c=4 interprets to '6 adult tickets and 4 child tickets were sold.'
example_2: Don't stop at the numbers — turn them into a sentence about the real problem.
key_rule: Always interpret the solution with correct units and context.
memory_hook: Turn the numbers back into a real-world sentence.

definition: The operation that reverses another, such as subtraction undoing addition or division undoing multiplication. Used to isolate the variable when solving equations.
example_1: For 2x+3=11: subtract 3 (inverse of +3), then divide by 2 (inverse of ×2).
example_2: Undo operations in reverse order — addition/subtraction first, then multiplication/division.
key_rule: Apply the same inverse operation to both sides to keep the equation balanced.
memory_hook: Undo each operation with its opposite.

definition: To get a variable by itself on one side of an equation using inverse operations. This is the goal when solving and the first step in substitution.
example_1: 3x+6=15: subtract 6 to get 3x=9, divide by 3 to get x=3.
example_2: Solve x+y=7 for y: y = 7-x.
key_rule: Use inverse operations to move every other term away from the variable.
memory_hook: Get the variable alone on one side.

definition: The least common denominator, the smallest multiple shared by all denominators. Multiplying every term of an equation by the LCD clears the fractions.
example_1: Denominators 2, 3, 6 have LCD = 6.
example_2: Multiply every term by the LCD, including whole numbers.
key_rule: Multiply every term, not just the fractions.
memory_hook: Clear the fractions by multiplying by the LCD.

definition: The same base appearing in two or more powers. Exponent rules such as adding or subtracting exponents only apply when the bases are identical.
example_1: 5³ · 5² = 5⁵ works because the bases match.
example_2: 2³ · 5⁴ stays as it is — different bases don't combine.
key_rule: Exponent rules only work when the bases match.
memory_hook: Same base — then the exponent rules apply.

definition: Terms that have the same variable raised to the same exponent. They can be combined by adding or subtracting their coefficients.
example_1: 3x + 2x = 5x (like terms).
example_2: 3x and 3x² are unlike terms — different exponents.
key_rule: Only combine like terms — the variable part must match exactly.
memory_hook: Same variable, same exponent — combine them.

definition: Adjusting numbers in scientific notation so their powers of ten are equal, which is required before adding or subtracting them.
example_2: Multiplying and dividing do NOT need matching exponents — only adding and subtracting do.
key_rule: Make the exponents match first, then add or subtract the coefficients.
memory_hook: Match the powers of ten before adding.

definition: A base raised to a negative power equals one over the base raised to the positive power. It indicates a reciprocal, not a negative value.
example_2: A negative exponent moves the power to the denominator.
key_rule: A negative exponent does not make the number negative — it signals a reciprocal.
memory_hook: Flip to the reciprocal, make the exponent positive.

definition: Occurs when solving an equation eliminates the variable and leaves a false statement. The two sides can never be equal, shown graphically by parallel lines.
example_1: 2x+5=2x+3 simplifies to 5=3 — false.
example_2: Graphically, the two lines are parallel and never intersect.
key_rule: A false statement with no variable left means no solution.
memory_hook: Variable disappears, statement is false — no solution.

definition: The point where the x-axis and y-axis intersect on a coordinate plane, with coordinates zero, zero. Proportional relationships always pass through the origin.
example_1: Every proportional graph passes through (0,0).
example_2: Buy 0 items, pay 0 dollars — that's the origin in context.
key_rule: Proportional relationships always pass through the origin; non-proportional ones don't.
memory_hook: Where the axes cross — (0,0).

definition: Lines in the same plane with equal slopes that never intersect. A system made of parallel lines has no solution.
example_1: y=2x+1 and y=2x+4 have the same slope (2) but different y-intercepts.
example_2: If solving the system gives a false statement, the lines are parallel.
key_rule: Parallel lines have the same slope but different y-intercepts.
memory_hook: Same slope, different starting point, never meet.

definition: A number that is the product of an integer used three times as a factor. Its cube root is a whole number.
example_1: 27 is a perfect cube because 3×3×3=27.
example_2: 1, 8, 27, 64, 125 are all perfect cubes.
key_rule: The cube root of a perfect cube is always a whole number.
memory_hook: An integer, cubed.

definition: Ten raised to an exponent. A positive exponent gives a large number and shifts the decimal right; a negative exponent gives a small number and shifts the decimal left.
example_1: 3.6 × 10⁵ = 360,000 (decimal moves 5 places right).
example_2: 3.6 × 10⁻⁴ = 0.00036 (decimal moves 4 places left).
key_rule: The exponent tells how many places, and which direction, the decimal moves.
memory_hook: Tells how many places the decimal moves.

definition: When raising a power to another power, keep the base and multiply the exponents. The quantity x to the a, raised to the b, equals x to the a times b.
example_2: Add exponents for the product rule, but multiply for power of a power.
key_rule: Multiply the exponents when raising a power to another power.
memory_hook: Keep the base, multiply the exponents.

definition: When multiplying powers with the same base, keep the base and add the exponents. x to the a times x to the b equals x to the a plus b.
example_1: x³ · x⁴ = x⁷.
example_2: Only works with the same base — x² · y⁴ does NOT combine.
key_rule: Add the exponents when multiplying powers with the same base.
memory_hook: Same base, multiplying — add the exponents.

definition: A relationship between two quantities with a constant ratio, written y equals k times x. Its graph is a straight line through the origin.
example_1: 3/1 = 6/2 = 9/3 = 3, so y is always 3 times x.
example_2: Test: does y divided by x give the same number every time?
key_rule: A proportional relationship's graph is always a straight line through the origin.
memory_hook: Same ratio every time, line through the origin.

definition: When dividing powers with the same base, keep the base and subtract the exponents. x to the a divided by x to the b equals x to the a minus b.
example_1: x⁷ ÷ x³ = x⁴.
example_2: Order matters — it's the top exponent minus the bottom exponent.
key_rule: Subtract the exponents (top minus bottom) when dividing powers with the same base.
memory_hook: Same base, dividing — subtract the exponents.

definition: The multiplicative inverse of a number, formed by flipping a fraction. A number times its reciprocal equals one.
example_2: To solve (2/3)x = 8, multiply both sides by the reciprocal 3/2 to get x = 12.
key_rule: To undo multiplying by a fraction, multiply by its reciprocal.
memory_hook: Flip the fraction to undo multiplication.

definition: To rewrite a number so the coefficient is between 1 and 10, adjusting the power of ten accordingly. Needed after multiplying or adding in scientific notation.
example_1: 42 × 10⁵ renormalizes to 4.2 × 10⁶.
example_2: 0.6 × 10⁵ renormalizes to 6 × 10⁴.
key_rule: If the coefficient is 10 or more, move the decimal left and add to the exponent; if less than 1, move right and subtract.
memory_hook: Adjust the coefficient back between 1 and 10.

definition: A method for finding slope by counting the vertical change (rise) and horizontal change (run) between two points on a line, then dividing rise by run.
example_1: Rise 3, run 4 gives a slope of 3/4.
example_2: Count the rise first (up/down), then the run (always to the right).
key_rule: Slope = rise ÷ run.
memory_hook: Count up, then count over.

definition: A way to write very large or very small numbers as a coefficient between 1 and 10 multiplied by a power of ten.
example_1: Speed of light: 300,000,000 m/s = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.
example_2: A red blood cell: 0.000007 m = 7 × 10⁻⁶ m.
key_rule: Positive exponent means a large number; negative exponent means a small number.
memory_hook: Big or small numbers, written compactly.

definition: A linear equation written as y equals m x plus b, where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept. It makes graphing a line quick and direct.
example_1: y = 2x + 3: slope 2, y-intercept 3.
example_2: Start at b on the y-axis, then use the slope to step to the next point.
key_rule: Start at the y-intercept, then use the slope to graph the line.
memory_hook: y = mx + b — slope and starting point in one equation.

definition: A value of the variable that makes an equation true. It can be verified by substituting it back into the original equation and checking that both sides are equal.
example_1: For 2x+3=11, x=4 checks out: 2(4)+3=11.
example_2: x=5 does NOT work: 2(5)+3=13≠11.
key_rule: Always check a solution by substituting it back into the original equation.
memory_hook: The value that makes both sides equal.

definition: The ordered pair of values that makes every equation in the system true at the same time, shown as the intersection point of the graphs.
example_1: (1,3) solves both y=2x+1 and y=-x+4.
example_2: Substitute the point into all equations to verify.
key_rule: The solution must satisfy every equation in the system.
memory_hook: The one point that works in every equation.

definition: A value that, multiplied by itself, gives the original number. It is the inverse of squaring and equals the side length of a square with that area.
example_2: The square root of a perfect square is a whole number; other square roots are irrational.
key_rule: Square root undoes squaring.
memory_hook: What number times itself gives this?

definition: A number written out in full with all of its digits and place values, as opposed to scientific notation. Also called ordinary or expanded numeric form.
example_1: 3.6 × 10⁵ in standard form is 360,000.
example_2: 7.2 × 10⁻³ in standard form is 0.0072.
key_rule: A positive exponent moves the decimal right (bigger); a negative exponent moves it left (smaller).
memory_hook: Every digit written out, no powers of ten.

definition: A method for solving a system by replacing one variable with an equivalent expression from another equation, reducing it to a single-variable equation.
example_1: y=2x and x+y=9: substitute to get x+2x=9, so x=3, y=6.
example_2: Best when a variable is already alone in one equation.
key_rule: Solve one equation for a variable, then substitute it into the other equation.
memory_hook: Swap one variable for its equivalent expression.

definition: A set of two or more equations with the same variables, solved together. The solution is the point that satisfies all equations at once.
example_1: The solution is where the graphs of both equations cross.
example_2: Solve by graphing, substitution, or elimination — all give the same intersection point.
key_rule: The solution to a system is the point that makes every equation true.
memory_hook: Two or more equations, one shared solution.

definition: A rate that compares a quantity to one unit of another, found by making the denominator equal to one. It equals the slope of a proportional graph.
example_1: 12 dollars for 4 pounds = 3 dollars per pound.
example_2: 180 miles in 3 hours = 60 miles per hour.
key_rule: On a proportional graph, the unit rate is the slope.
memory_hook: The amount for exactly one unit.

definition: Any nonzero base raised to the power of zero equals one. This follows from the pattern of dividing by the base and from the quotient rule.
example_2: Exception: 0⁰ is undefined.
key_rule: Anything (except 0) to the zero power equals 1.
memory_hook: Any nonzero base to the zero power is 1.

definition: A triangle is classified by comparing the sum of the squares of the two shorter sides to the square of the longest side. Greater means acute, less means obtuse, equal means right.
example_2: If a²+b² = c², the triangle is right.
key_rule: Plug the longest side in as c, then compare a²+b² to c².
memory_hook: Compare the squares to classify the triangle.

definition: A pair of angles between two lines and on opposite sides of a transversal. When the lines are parallel, these angles are equal.
example_1: If one alternate interior angle is 65°, the other is also 65°.
example_2: They form a Z-pattern between parallel lines.
key_rule: With parallel lines, alternate interior angles are always equal.
memory_hook: Inside the lines, opposite sides, equal angles.

definition: The number of degrees a figure is turned about the center of rotation. Common angles include 90, 180, and 270 degrees.
example_1: A 180-degree rotation about the origin sends (x,y) to (-x,-y).
example_2: Counterclockwise is positive; clockwise is negative.
key_rule: The angle of rotation tells how far the figure turns.
memory_hook: How many degrees it spins.

definition: The line over which a figure is reflected, acting as a mirror. Each point and its image are equidistant from this line on opposite sides.
example_1: Reflecting over the x-axis: (x,y) → (x,-y).
example_2: Reflecting over the y-axis: (x,y) → (-x,y).
key_rule: Every point and its image are the same distance from the axis, on opposite sides.
memory_hook: The mirror line the figure flips across.

definition: The two directions of rotation. Counterclockwise is taken as the positive direction, opposite to the motion of clock hands.
example_1: Unless told otherwise, rotations are counterclockwise (CCW).
example_2: A 90° CW turn equals a 270° CCW turn.
key_rule: Counterclockwise is positive; clockwise is negative.
memory_hook: Two directions a figure can spin.

definition: The fixed point from which a figure is enlarged or reduced. Each point moves along a ray from this center, scaled by the scale factor.
example_1: If the center is the origin, multiply each coordinate by the scale factor.
example_2: Distance to the image = scale factor × distance to the preimage.
key_rule: Every point moves along a ray from the center of dilation.
memory_hook: The fixed point the figure scales from.

definition: The fixed point about which a figure is turned. Every point of the figure maintains its distance from this center during the rotation.
example_1: The center of rotation can be the origin, a vertex, or any other point.
example_2: Every point of the figure stays the same distance from the center while turning.
key_rule: The center of rotation is the one point that does not move.
memory_hook: The fixed point everything turns around.

definition: A pair of angles between two lines and on the same side of a transversal. When the lines are parallel, these angles are supplementary, summing to 180 degrees.
example_1: If one co-interior angle is 110°, the other is 70° (110+70=180).
example_2: Also called same-side interior angles.
key_rule: Co-interior angles are supplementary — they add to 180°.
memory_hook: Same side, inside the lines, add to 180°.

definition: A sequence of two or more transformations applied one after another. The final image is labeled with double-prime marks.
example_1: Translate, then reflect: ABC → A'B'C' → A''B''C''.
example_2: A composition of rigid motions is still a rigid motion — the image stays congruent.
key_rule: Each transformation in the sequence adds a prime mark to the label.
memory_hook: One transformation, then another.

definition: A three-dimensional solid with a circular base narrowing to a single point. Its volume is one-third times pi times the radius squared times the height.
example_1: V = (1/3)πr²h.
example_2: A cone's volume is exactly one-third of a cylinder with the same base and height.
key_rule: Cone volume is one-third of the matching cylinder's volume.
memory_hook: One-third of a cylinder's volume.

definition: Having exactly the same size and shape. Two figures are congruent if a sequence of rigid motions maps one onto the other, making all corresponding parts equal.
example_1: If △ABC ≅ △DEF, then AB=DE, BC=EF, CA=FD.
example_2: Congruent means same size AND same shape; similar means same shape, possibly different size.
key_rule: A rigid motion (slide, flip, turn) maps congruent figures exactly onto each other.
memory_hook: Same size, same shape, exactly.

definition: If the sum of the squares of two sides equals the square of the third, then the triangle is a right triangle.
example_1: Sides 6,8,10: 36+64=100=10² — right triangle.
example_2: Sides 4,5,7: 16+25=41≠49 — NOT a right triangle.
key_rule: Use the converse to check if a triangle is right by testing a²+b²=c².
memory_hook: Check the equation to prove it's a right triangle.

definition: An ordered pair of numbers that specifies a point's position on the plane. The first value is the horizontal x-coordinate, the second is the vertical y-coordinate.
example_1: (4, 3) is not the same as (3, 4) — order matters.
example_2: Always x, then y.
key_rule: The coordinates give a point's exact address on the plane.
memory_hook: x first, y second — a point's address.

definition: Angles in matching positions where a transversal crosses two lines. When the lines are parallel, corresponding angles are equal.
example_1: If one corresponding angle is 110°, the other is also 110°.
example_2: They sit in the same position at each crossing, like top-right and top-right.
key_rule: Parallel lines make corresponding angles equal.
memory_hook: Same position, each intersection, equal angles.

definition: A three-dimensional solid with two parallel circular bases connected by a curved surface. Its volume equals pi times the radius squared times the height.
example_1: V = π(3)²(5) = 45π ≈ 141.4 cubic units.
example_2: Volume = area of the circular base times height.
key_rule: Cylinder volume is the circular base area times the height.
memory_hook: Circle base, straight up, times height.

definition: A transformation that resizes a figure by a scale factor from a center point. The image is similar to the original, with the same shape but a different size.
example_1: From the origin with scale factor 2: (x,y) → (2x, 2y).
example_2: Scale factor greater than 1 enlarges; between 0 and 1 shrinks.
key_rule: Dilation is NOT rigid — the size changes, but the shape stays similar.
memory_hook: Resize from a center point.

definition: The straight-line length between two points on a coordinate plane, found by applying the Pythagorean theorem to the horizontal and vertical changes.
example_1: From (0,0) to (4,5) with legs 4 and... wait: distance = √(3²+4²) = √25 = 5.
example_2: Make a right triangle from the horizontal and vertical changes; the distance is the hypotenuse.
key_rule: Use the Pythagorean theorem on the horizontal and vertical legs.
memory_hook: Make a right triangle, then find the hypotenuse.

definition: Notation using two prime marks to label a figure's vertices after a second transformation, as in A double prime. It shows the image of an image.
example_1: A → A' (after first transformation) → A'' (after second).
example_2: Original has no mark; one transformation gets one prime; two get double prime.
key_rule: Each transformation adds one more prime mark.
memory_hook: Two transformations, two prime marks.

definition: The longest side of a right triangle, located opposite the right angle. In the Pythagorean theorem it is the side labeled c.
example_1: In a²+b²=c², the hypotenuse is always c.
example_2: It's always the side directly across from the 90° angle.
key_rule: The hypotenuse is always the longest side and always opposite the right angle.
memory_hook: The longest side, across from the right angle.

definition: The resulting figure after a transformation is applied to the preimage. Its vertices are labeled with primed letters such as A prime, B prime, C prime.
example_1: After transforming ABC, the result is A', B', C'.
example_2: Read A' as 'A prime'.
key_rule: The image is the output figure — what you get after transforming.
memory_hook: What you get after transforming.

definition: Either of the two sides of a right triangle that form the right angle. The legs are labeled a and b in the Pythagorean theorem.
example_1: In a²+b²=c², a and b are the legs.
example_2: To find a leg, rearrange: a² = c² - b².
key_rule: The legs are the two shorter sides that meet at the right angle.
memory_hook: One of the two sides forming the right angle.

definition: The two perpendicular sides of a right triangle. When finding distance on a coordinate plane, the legs are the horizontal and vertical changes between the points.
example_1: Horizontal leg = 6, vertical leg = 8 → distance = √(6²+8²) = 10.
example_2: The legs form the right angle; the distance is the hypotenuse.
key_rule: On a coordinate plane, the legs are the change in x and the change in y.
memory_hook: Horizontal and vertical changes, forming the right angle.

definition: The original figure before a transformation is applied. Its vertices are labeled with plain letters, while the transformed image uses primed letters.
example_1: ABC is the preimage; A'B'C' is the image.
example_2: Pre- means 'before' — the preimage comes first.
key_rule: The preimage is the input figure — the original, before any transformation.
memory_hook: The original, before anything happens to it.

definition: In a right triangle, the sum of the squares of the two legs equals the square of the hypotenuse. a squared plus b squared equals c squared.
example_2: Works ONLY for right triangles.
key_rule: c is always the hypotenuse — the longest side, across from the right angle.
memory_hook: a² + b² = c², for right triangles only.

definition: A transformation that flips a figure over a line of reflection, producing a mirror image. Each point and its image are equidistant from the line.
example_1: (x, y) → (-x, y) when reflecting over the y-axis.
example_2: The image is congruent but reversed.
key_rule: Each point and its image are the same distance from the line, on opposite sides.
memory_hook: A flip over a line, like a mirror.

definition: An angle that measures exactly 90°, forming a square corner. It is marked with a small square symbol rather than an arc.
example_1: The corner of a page is a right angle.
example_2: Acute < 90°, right = 90°, obtuse > 90°.
key_rule: A right angle is marked with a small square, not an arc.
memory_hook: Exactly 90°, a perfect square corner.

definition: A transformation that preserves distances and angles, producing a congruent image. Translations, reflections, and rotations are rigid motions; dilations are not.
example_1: A translation slides a figure without changing its size or shape.
example_2: A dilation is NOT rigid — its size changes.
key_rule: Rigid motions always produce a congruent image.
memory_hook: Slides, flips, and turns — size and shape stay the same.

definition: A transformation that turns a figure about a fixed center point by a specified angle and direction. The image is congruent to the original.
example_1: 90° CCW about the origin: (x,y) → (-y,x).
example_2: Size and shape stay the same — only the orientation changes.
key_rule: A rotation is a rigid motion — the image stays congruent to the original.
memory_hook: A turn around a fixed center point.

definition: The ratio of a length in the image to the corresponding length in the original figure. It tells how much a dilation enlarges or reduces a figure.
example_1: A side of 4 becomes 8 → scale factor = 8/4 = 2.
example_2: k > 1 enlarges; k = 1 keeps the same size; 0 < k < 1 shrinks.
key_rule: Scale factor compares the image's length to the original's length.
memory_hook: How many times bigger or smaller.

definition: Figures with the same shape but not necessarily the same size. Their corresponding angles are equal and corresponding sides are proportional.
example_1: A triangle scaled by 2: all sides double, all angles stay equal.
example_2: Similar figures use the symbol ~.
key_rule: Similar figures have equal angles and proportional sides.
memory_hook: Same shape, sizes can differ.

definition: A perfectly round three-dimensional solid where every surface point is equidistant from the center. Its volume is four-thirds times pi times the radius cubed.
example_1: V = (4/3)π(3)³ = 36π ≈ 113.1 cubic units.
example_2: Every point on the surface is the same distance r from the center.
key_rule: Sphere volume uses r cubed, since a sphere fills three dimensions.
memory_hook: Perfectly round, volume uses r cubed.

definition: A transformation that slides every point of a figure the same distance in the same direction. The image is congruent to the original.
example_1: (x, y) → (x+5, y+3) slides right 5 and up 3.
example_2: Size, shape, and orientation stay the same.
key_rule: A translation is a rigid motion — the image stays congruent.
memory_hook: A slide — everything moves the same way.

definition: A line that intersects two or more other lines. When it crosses parallel lines, it creates pairs of angles with special relationships.
example_1: A transversal cutting two parallel lines forms 8 angles.
example_2: Angle 1 and angle 5 are corresponding; angle 3 and angle 6 are alternate interior.
key_rule: With parallel lines, a transversal creates equal or supplementary angle pairs.
memory_hook: A line that crosses two parallel lines.

definition: A quantity with both magnitude and direction, drawn as an arrow. In translations it specifies how far and in what direction each point moves.
example_1: Vector <5, 3> means move 5 right and 3 up.
example_2: The first number is horizontal move; the second is vertical move.
key_rule: A translation vector tells both distance and direction.
memory_hook: An arrow showing how far and which way to slide.

definition: The amount of three-dimensional space a solid occupies, measured in cubic units. Cylinders, cones, and spheres each have their own volume formula involving pi.
example_1: Cylinder: V = πr²h.
example_2: Cone: V = (1/3)πr²h. Sphere: V = (4/3)πr³.
key_rule: Cylinder, cone, and sphere all use pi and the radius, but the cone has 1/3 and the sphere uses r cubed.
memory_hook: Space inside a solid, measured in cubic units.

definition: The relationship between two variables shown in a scatter plot, described by its direction (positive or negative), form (linear or nonlinear), and strength.
example_1: Points trending both up together show positive association.
example_2: Points scattered randomly show no association.
key_rule: Association can be linear or nonlinear, strong or weak.
memory_hook: How two variables move together.

definition: A relative frequency calculated within a single row or column of a two-way table, showing the proportion of one category given another.
example_1: Of those who like dogs (35 total), 20 also like cats: 20/35 ≈ 57%.
example_2: This is out of the row total, not the grand total.
key_rule: A conditional frequency answers 'given this category, what fraction are also...?'
memory_hook: A fraction within one row or column, not the whole table.

definition: The count in a single inner cell of a two-way table, representing how many data values fall into one specific combination of both categories.
example_1: 20 people like both dogs AND cats — that's the joint frequency.
example_2: Joint = an inner cell; marginal = a total on the edge.
key_rule: A joint frequency is a count inside the table, not a total.
memory_hook: One exact combination, counted.

definition: An equation of a straight line used to describe the relationship in a data set and to make predictions. Its slope and intercept are interpreted in real-world context.
example_1: y = 8x + 50: predict for 5 hours → y = 8(5)+50 = 90 points.
example_2: Slope 8 means each hour adds about 8 points; intercept 50 is the predicted score with 0 hours.
key_rule: Use the linear model to describe the trend and predict new outputs.
memory_hook: An equation that describes the data's trend.

definition: A straight line drawn through scatter-plot data to model the overall trend, positioned so the points are balanced as closely as possible around it.
example_1: The line follows the data's overall direction, even without touching every point.
example_2: About as many points fall above the line as below it.
key_rule: The line of best fit does not need to touch every point — it captures the overall direction.
memory_hook: The line that best follows the trend.

definition: A row or column total in a two-way table, found in the margins. It gives the overall count for a single category, ignoring the other variable.
example_1: 35 people like dogs in total (20 who also like cats + 15 who don't).
example_2: Marginal = edge total (one variable); joint = inner cell (both variables).
key_rule: A marginal frequency is a total in the margins of the table.
memory_hook: A total on the edge, for one category.

definition: A measure of how well a line or curve matches the data it represents. Points clustered closely around the line indicate a strong fit; widely scattered points indicate a weak fit.
example_1: Points close to the line show a strong fit.
example_2: Points scattered widely show a weak fit.
key_rule: The smaller the gaps between points and the line, the better the fit.
memory_hook: How closely the line matches the data.

definition: A data point that differs markedly from the overall pattern of a data set. It lies far from the other points and can strongly affect summaries and trend lines.
example_1: Most points follow the trend, but one point stands apart.
example_2: An outlier can come from a special case or a measurement error.
key_rule: One unusual point can change your conclusions — always look for outliers.
memory_hook: A point that doesn't fit the pattern.

definition: An estimate of an output value made by substituting a new input into a data model's equation. Predictions are most reliable within the range of the observed data.
example_1: For 6 hours: y=8(6)+50=98 points predicted.
example_2: Predicting far outside the data range is risky — the trend may not continue.
key_rule: Plug the new input into the model's equation to predict the output.
memory_hook: Use the model to estimate a new value.

definition: The difference between an observed data value and the value predicted by the line of best fit, measured vertically. Small residuals indicate the line fits the data well.
example_1: Actual = 80, predicted = 74, residual = 80-74 = 6.
example_2: Points above the line have positive residuals; points below have negative residuals.
key_rule: Residual = actual value minus predicted value.
memory_hook: How far off the prediction was, for one point.

definition: A graph of paired numerical data in which each point represents one observation. It is used to reveal relationships, trends, or clusters between two variables.
example_1: Each dot is one data pair (hours studied, test score).
example_2: As hours go up, scores tend to go up — a positive trend.
key_rule: Look at the cloud of points as a whole — its shape and direction tell the story.
memory_hook: Paired data, shown as points, to reveal a pattern.

definition: A table that displays counts of data classified by two categorical variables, with inner cells showing combinations and margins showing row and column totals.
example_1: Rows for 'Likes Dogs/Dislikes Dogs', columns for 'Likes Cats/Dislikes Cats'.
example_2: Inner cells are joint counts; edges are marginal totals.
key_rule: A two-way table sorts data by two categorical variables at once.
memory_hook: Counts organized by two categories.
